# Diagnostic Invasive Procedures in Diffuse Infiltrative Lung Diseases

Venerino Poletti a, b Marco Chilosi c Dario Olivieriᵇ

a Dipartimento di Malattie dell’Apparato Respiratorio e del Torace, Ospedale GB Morgagni, Forlı

b Dipartimento di Scienze Cliniche, Sezione di Clinica Pneumologica, Universita di Parma, Parma, e

c Dipartimento di Patologica, Universita di Verona, Verona, Italia

# Key Words

transbronchial lung biopsy, transbronchial needle aspiration and biopsy or surgical lung biopsy are necessary. Diffuse infiltrative lung disease W Interstitial lung disease W Immunocompromised patient W Bronchoalveolar lavage W Transbronchial lung biopsy

# Abstract

The diagnosis of infiltrative diffuse lung disease may require invasive procedures after all noninvasive tools have failed. The clinical context in which these diseases develop and the radiological patterns are crucial for defining the timing and the methods to be used. Immunocompromised hosts are usually acutely ill with fever, cough, shortness of breath, and often with progressive hypoxemia. In this context a prompt diagnosis is necessary to decrease mortality. Bronchoalveolar lavage [especially in cases that show ground-glass attenuation or alveolar opacification in high-resolution CT scan (HRCT)] is the most important invasive procedure allowing the identification of infectious agents, neoplastic elements and characteristic cytological and phenotypical profiles (for drug injury) in the majority of cases. Less frequently surgical lung biopsy is, however, still relevant in cases with idiopathic interstitial pneumonias. In this article invasive diagnostic procedures in patients with diffuse lung infiltrates are discussed from the perspective of their clinical context and their imaging characteristics.

# Introduction

The diagnostic approach to diffuse lung disease is a step-by-step process and invasive procedures are considered only when other techniques are inconclusive and tissue, cellular elements or alveolar fluid are deemed necessary for a definitive diagnosis or for therapeutic decisions. The key points in discussing the role of these procedures in the clinical setting of diffuse lung disease are.

pages.md:
# Immunocompromised Hosts

Immunocompromised patients who develop diffuse lung disease are usually acutely ill with fever, cough, shortness of breath, and often with progressive hypoxemia [9, 10, 12]. In these circumstances it can be difficult clinically to distinguish neoplastic infiltration of the lungs or lung involvement due to collagen vascular disease or vasculitis from overhydration, heart failure, opportunist infection and from the toxic effects of antineoplastic chemotherapy. Radiation therapy, graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), acute and chronic rejection, and idiopathic pulmonary disorders (i.e. alveolar proteinosis in neutropenic subjects, diffuse alveolar damage, organizing pneumonia) may also contribute to the development of pulmonary complications in this clinical setting. The prognosis of immunocompromised patients with lung infiltrates is grim when mechanical ventilation is required [13]. A prompt diagnosis seems, therefore, to be crucial for a better survival [14].

Induced sputum has a role only in HIV-positive patients because of its high diagnostic yield in Pneumocystis carinii (or Pneumocystis jirovecii) pneumonia and tuberculosis [15, 16]. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL), especially when the bronchoscope is guided by HRCT scan findings performed a few hours before, is generally of value in diagnosing opportunist infections, alveolar proteinosis, alveolar hemorrhage and capillaritis, lymphomatous or leukemic lung infiltration, carcinomatous lymphangitis, disseminated hematogenous lung metastases, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, eosinophilic pneumonitis or diffuse alveolar damage due to drugs or radiation [17–20]. Specific findings are diagnostic per se: P. carinii, acid fast bacilli, direct immunofluorescent test for Legionella, intranuclear or intracytoplasmic viral inclusions, Strongyloides stercoralis or other parasitic infections, neoplastic cells, intracytoplasmic hemosiderin and a bloody fluid recovered in alveolar hemorrhage, eosinophilia in eosinophilic pneumonia, intracytoplasmic macrovacuoles and intra- and extracellular oil red-positive drops in lipoidic pneumonia, amorphous extracellular PAS-positive and.

# Alcian-negative material in alveolar lipoproteinosis, reactive type II pneumocytes collected in pseudopapillae around amorphous extracellular material in diffuse alveolar damage

(table 1; fig. 1). The detection of bacteria or viruses or fungi often after having used more sophisticated microbiological investigations is not per se diagnostic of pneumonia. In these cases the distinction between pneumonia or colonization is based on the radiologic aspects and the clinical background [19, 21].

Nonspecific findings in BAL fluid may be used as an adjunct to diagnosis together with an extensive clinical and roentgenological investigation (table 2) [10, 22–24].

In drug- or radiation-related toxicity besides hyperplastic/dysplastic changes of type II pneumocytes and negative microbiological investigations, any type of alveolitis has been described in BAL fluid. However, morphologically activated CD8+ cells indicating a hypersensitivity reaction are the most frequent and suggestive occurrence. Cellular interstitial pneumonitis as clinicopathological manifestation of GVHD in the lungs may be inferred on the basis of negative pertinent microbiological investigations and CD8+ cell lymphocytosis in BAL fluid [24–26].

Application of deep sedation and use of the laryngeal mask appear to be a safe and effective alternative to intubation for accomplishing fiberoptic bronchoscopy with BAL in patients (adults and children) with suspected pneumonia and severe hypoxemia [27–29]. Protected brushing or lavage has a lower diagnostic yield and specificity because they do not allow to correlate microbiological data with the cytological profile detectable in the respiratory airways and brushing is associated with a higher incidence of side effects [19].

HRCT patterns that have been shown to predict a high diagnostic yield of BAL procedure are [10, 18, 23, 26]: alveolar and/or ground-glass opacities (nonspecific patterns observed more frequently in infections, drug-induced toxicity, organizing pneumonia, alveolar hemorrhage and diffuse alveolar damage), ‘tree in bud pattern’ (more typically observed in bronchiolitis and peribronchiolitis of infectious cause, i.e. tuberculosis, lobular pneumonitis), nodular or reticulo-nodular pattern with a perilymphatic distribution (typically observed in lymphomatous infiltration and carcinomatous lymphangitis), and excavated nodules (usually of infectious nature) [24, 30]. A conclusive diagnosis is not obtained in the minority by BAL fluid analysis. In these cases transbronchial lung biopsy or even open lung biopsy has to be considered.

Transbronchial lung biopsy (TBB) is a procedure where pneumothorax is a not very frequent morbidity and significant bleeding a more dangerous and frequent side effect [32]. In cases in which a TBB is deemed necessary and is feasible (absence of significant coagulopathy, platelet 1 50,000) bronchoscopy procedures should be performed under general anesthesia through a rigid bronchoscope or an oro- or nasotracheal tube under fluoroscopic control in order to better control ventilation and bleeding [32]. In this way the bioptic forceps are not passed through the operating channel of the fiberoptic bronchoscope and therefore, the samples, frequently larger than the tip of the forceps, are not crushed (fig. 2). Combined BAL and TBB may also be performed safely in mechanically ventilated patients [33].

cases. White et al. [37] reported their experience in hematological patients. A specific diagnosis was found in 41 (62%) of the biopsies. The factor most predictive of finding a specific diagnosis was the presence of a focal rather than a diffuse radiographic abnormality. Specific pulmonary diagnoses found were inflammatory diseases in 23% of cases, infections in 21%, and malignancy in 18%. Bronchiolitis obliterans with organizing pneumonia was the most common inflammatory disorder and fungi and bacteria were the most frequent infectious pathogens. Neutropenic patients or those on mechanical ventilation had a low chance of finding a specific diagnosis. Having received pulmonary toxic chemotherapy in the 6 months before the biopsy was associated with finding a nonspecific lung injury [36]. Changes in therapy were made in 57% of patients after biopsy results, but in 69% of those with a specific diagnosis and survival at 30 and 90 days was increased in those with specific rather than a nonspecific pulmonary diagnosis [37]. Complications occurred in 13% of the patients, including 5 patients who required mechanical ventilation after the procedure; one death was associated with the biopsy. The risk was increased in those with less than 50,000 platelets. Complications were similar with VATS compared with thoracotomy.

Some authors have claimed that the lack of impact of invasive diagnostic techniques on survival was the major reason for not performing them in immunocompromised patients [38]. However, a significant decrease in mortality in those patients with an infectious etiology in whom an early diagnosis (7 days from the initiation of the pulmonary infiltrates) caused a change in treatment (29%) compared with those in whom this diagnosis was obtained after 7 days (71%) was found by Rano et al. [39]. The same group has shown that requirement for mechanical ventilation, an APACHE (acute physiology and chronic health evaluation) II score of 120 and again a delay of 15 days in establishing a specific diagnosis were the variables associated with mortality at the multivariate analysis in immunocompromised patients with lung infiltrates.

The added value of TBB in patients undergoing BAL remains contentious. In a retrospective study of immunosuppressed patients TBB was more sensitive than BAL (77 vs. 48% in HIV disease, 55 vs. 20% in hematological malignancy, 57 vs. 27% in renal transplant recipients) and there were few serious complications [40]. In patients with HIV infection it has been argued that a negative BAL result should prompt a repeat BAL with TBB at the most abnormal site; this approach has a diagnostic yield of 90% for nodules or focal infiltrates [41]. In a retrospective study, induced sputum has a subordinate role, being diagnostic in cases of lipoidic pneumonia, eosinophilic pneumonia and, only in skilled hands and in research centers, in.

BAL is nowadays considered a milestone in the diagnostic workup of immunocompetent subjects having a diffuse lung disease. There are a few lung diseases in this context in which BAL may provide specific findings that, if present, obviate the need of a biopsy: alveolar proteinosis, dust exposure, eosinophilic lung disease, alveolar hemorrhage, diffuse alveolar damage whatever the clinical context may be (acute interstitial pneumonia, acute exacerbation of IPF, acute respiratory distress syndrome), Langerhans cell histiocytosis, B cell lymphoma of the MALT type (fig. 5), carcinomatous lymphangitis, bronchoalveolar cell carcinoma, lipoidic pneumonia, and rarer.

# Diffuse Infiltrative Lung Diseases

CD19 PE 102 FL2-H 102

Fig. 5. a BAL. Cytofluorometric analysis showing a significant increase of B lymphocytes (CD19-positive cells). b BAL. Cytofluorometric analysis showing a monotypic expression of light chains (lambda chains). Diagnosis: B cell MALT-type lymphoma in the lung.

Disorders such as congenital metabolic diseases (Gaucher disease, Hermansky-Pudlak disease) [22, 45–49]. It is noteworthy that many of these disorders are characterized from a pathological point of view by the accumulation of diagnostic cells or extracellular material in the alveolar spaces (the so-called group of alveolar filling diseases) and from a roentgenological point of view by alveolar opacification or ground-glass attenuation on HRCT scan film. The diagnostic yield is very high (almost 100% in alveolar proteinosis and eosinophilic lung disease) except for Langerhans cell histiocytosis in which more than 4% of CD1a+ cells are detected in less than 40% of cases in our institution (and usually in cases with typical nodular and cystic lesions in HRCT scan).

Generous and numerous TBB specimens may document the presence of this triad and, therefore, be diagnostic. In a number of more numerous diffuse lung diseases, BAL findings are not specific but can be used as an adjunct to diagnosis (table 3). In these conditions BAL analysis may be the key to diagnosis, together with a careful clinical/radiological evaluation, making a biopsy unnecessary in many cases [22]. However, if despite this thorough investigation the diagnosis remains unclear, a biopsy should be considered as the further diagnostic step.

TBB may be considered as the first choice. Andersen et al. [50] are given credit for the first description of TBB following their observation that bronchial biopsy specimens of small airways harvested during the course of rigid bronchoscopy often contained lung parenchyma. TBB forceps reach lung tissue through the bronchial routes and therefore specimens so obtained come from the centrilobular regions [51]. The disorders that are centered around terminal and respiratory bronchioles [respiratory bronchiolitis (RB), tuberculosis, lobular infectious pneumonia, cellular bronchiolitis] or significantly involve these structures (organizing pneumonia) or are distributed along the lymphatic routes (sarcoidosis, carcinomatous lymphangitis) may be easily sampled by the forceps. In ‘lymphangitic’ disorders bronchial biopsies can also contribute to increasing the diagnostic accuracy [11, 51]. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis in its subacute stage is histologically defined by the presence of peribronchiolar lymphocytic infiltration (cellular bronchiolitis), small ill-defined granulomas, and organizing pneumonia [52].

# 3. Disease with nonspecific but characteristic BAL profile

| Disease                                                           | Features                                                                                      |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Sarcoidosis                                                       | Increased CD4/CD8 ratio a                                                                     |
| Hypersensitivity pneumonitis                                      | Increased total cell count, lymphocytosis, mast cells, plasma cells, decreased CD4/CD8 ratioᵇ |
| Organizing pneumonia                                              | Lymphocytosis, scattered neutrophils and eosinophils, plasma cells, decreased CD4/CD8 ratioᵇ  |
| Nonspecific interstitial pneumonitis (cellular variant)           | Lymphocytosis (decreased CD4/CD8 ratio)                                                       |
| Respiratory bronchiolitis-ILD/desquamative interstitial pneumonia | Increased total cell count, brown macrophages, slight increase of neutrophils                 |
| Giant cell interstitial pneumonitis                               | Multinucleated giant cell, ‘cannibalistic’ figures                                            |
| Bacterial pneumonia                                               | Neutrophils, apoptotic nuclei, intracytoplasmic micro-organisms                               |

ILD = Interstitial lung disease.

a CD4/CD8 ratio may also be increased in berylliosis, tuberculosis, asbestosis, Crohn’s disease, rheumatoid arthritis.

b The differential diagnosis between hypersensitivity pneumonitis and organizing pneumonitis is often not feasible on the basis of BAL features only.

From the morphological point of view the pattern called nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP), cellular variant is quite characteristic (maintenance of pulmonary architecture, interstitial thickening due to mononuclear cell infiltration, cuboidal type II metaplasia) but it is clinically nonspecific being observed in cases of collagen vascular disease (inflammatory myositis in particular), in patients with drug-related toxicity, in GVHD-related lung damage, and, in a minority of cases, it is of unknown cause (idiopathic NSIP) [56]. Surgical lung biopsy is considered the best way to obtain enough tissue to demonstrate this pattern. However, generous TBB may provide good specimens for a morphological diagnosis. This pathological diagnosis is acceptable in specific clinical settings (polymyositis-dermatomyositis-related interstitial lung disease, drug toxicity) but it might also be of clinical value in cases of unknown etiology [57].

RB is at last easily recognizable in TBB specimens and nowadays the utility of surgical lung biopsy in the diagnosis of RB-associated interstitial lung disease is disputable and probably ethically not advisable. The role of the number of specimens obtained and of the size of the forceps used has not been evaluated in detail in the literature. Descombes et al. [54] reviewed the histological and clinical data of 530 consecutive TBBs performed in 516 immunocompetent patients, having shown that there is a direct correlation between the number of samples obtained per TBB and the overall diagnostic yield (i.e. 38% with one to three tissue fragments vs. 69% with six to ten, p ! 0.01). They recommend that at least five to six specimens per TBB should be taken, the optimal number of specimens to optimize the diagnostic yield being, however, seven to ten. Curley et al. [58] reported that larger biopsy specimens were more likely to contain diagnostic tissue (r = 0.29, p = 0.001). Cup forceps retrieved smaller pieces of tissue (p = 0.007) and were less likely to obtain diagnostic tissue (p = 0.06). Specimens that floated were no more likely to be diagnostic or abnormal than specimens that sank (p ! 0.05). Loube et al. [59] prospectively compared the diagnostic yield of transbronchial biopsies using large and small forceps (cup sizes, 3 ! 2 ! 0.9 vs. 2 ! 1.5 ! 0.6 mm, respectively). Large forceps obtained significantly more tissue than did small forceps [20 of 27 patients (74%) vs. 5 of 27 patients (19%), p ! 0.005]. Also, large forceps obtained significantly more alveolar tissue than did small forceps [16 of 22 patients (73%) vs. 6 of 22 patients (27%), p ! 0.05, with no alveolar tissue obtained in 5 patients]. If larger forceps are not withdrawn through the operative channel, crash artifacts may also be avoided. Becker et al. [60] and Casoni et al.

Surgical lung biopsy is considered the best way to obtain enough tissue to demonstrate this pattern. However, generous TBB may provide good specimens for a morphological diagnosis. This pathological diagnosis is acceptable in specific clinical settings (polymyositis-dermatomyositis-related interstitial lung disease, drug toxicity) but it might also be of clinical value in cases of unknown etiology [57].

items.md: RB is at last easily recognizable in TBB specimens and nowadays the utility of surgical lung biopsy in the diagnosis of RB-associated interstitial lung disease is disputable and probably ethically not advisable. The role of the number of specimens obtained and of the size of the forceps used has not been evaluated in detail in the literature. Descombes et al. [54] reviewed the histological and clinical data of 530 consecutive TBBs performed in 516 immunocompetent patients, having shown that there is a direct correlation between the number of samples obtained per TBB and the overall diagnostic yield (i.e. 38% with one to three tissue fragments vs. 69% with six to ten, p ! 0.01). They recommend that at least five to six specimens per TBB should be taken, the optimal number of specimens to optimize the diagnostic yield being, however, seven to ten. Curley et al. [58] reported that larger biopsy specimens were more likely to contain diagnostic tissue (r = 0.29, p = 0.001). Cup forceps retrieved smaller pieces of tissue (p = 0.007) and were less likely to obtain diagnostic tissue (p = 0.06). Specimens that floated were no more likely to be diagnostic or abnormal than specimens that sank (p ! 0.05). Loube et al. [59] prospectively compared the diagnostic yield of transbronchial biopsies using large and small forceps (cup sizes, 3 ! 2 ! 0.9 vs. 2 ! 1.5 ! 0.6 mm, respectively). Large forceps obtained significantly more tissue than did small forceps [20 of 27 patients (74%) vs. 5 of 27 patients (19%), p ! 0.005]. Also, large forceps obtained significantly more alveolar tissue than did small forceps [16 of 22 patients (73%) vs. 6 of 22 patients (27%), p ! 0.05, with no alveolar tissue obtained in 5 patients]. If larger forceps are not withdrawn through the operative channel, crash artifacts may also be avoided. Becker et al. [60] and Casoni et al.

# Table 4. Imunohistochemical staining procedures useful to reach a definitive diagnosis on small lung bioptic specimens

| Immunophenotypical marker | Recognized cell type                               | Application                                                     |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Alpha smooth-muscle actin | Bronchiolar and vessel wall, myofibroblasts        | Vascular abnormality, fibroblast foci, Masson’s bodies          |
| Bcl-10                    | Abnormal expression (nuclear)                      | MALT lymphoma                                                   |
| Beta catenin (nuclear)    | Wnt pathway activation                             | UIP                                                             |
| CD1a                      | Langerhans cells                                   | Langerhans cell histiocytosis                                   |
| Langerin                  | Langerhans cells                                   | Langerhans cell histiocytosis                                   |
| CD3                       | T cells                                            | Alveolitis                                                      |
| CD4                       | T cell subset                                      | Alveolitis CD4+                                                 |
| CD8                       | T cell subset                                      | Often increased in HP                                           |
| CD34                      | Endothelial cells                                  | Vascular abnormalities                                          |
| CD20                      | B cells                                            | LIP, B cell lymphomas                                           |
| CD68                      | Macrophages                                        | DIP, panbronchiolitis                                           |
| Cytokeratin 8/18          | Epithelial cells (bronchiolar, alveolar)           | Alveolar hyperplasia                                            |
| Cytokeratin 5             | Bronchiolar basal cells                            | UIP, bronchiolization, squamous metaplasia                      |
| EBV EBER RNA              | EBV infection                                      | Lymphomatoid granulomatosis, nasal-type T cell lymphoma         |
| Granzyme                  | Activated cytotoxic T cells                        | T cell lymphoma                                                 |
| Tenascin                  | Extracellular matrix component                     | Ongoing fibrosis, fibroblast foci in UIP, Masson’s bodies in OP |
| ¢N-p63                    | Basal cells in bronchial and bronchiolar epithelia | Bronchiolar proliferation in UIP                                |

UIP = Usual interstitial pneumonitis; HP = hypersensitivity pneumonitis; LIP = lymphocytic interstitial pneumonitis; DIP = desquamative interstitial pneumonitis; OP = organizing pneumonia; EBV = Epstein-Barr virus; EBER RNA = encoded small nuclear early region RNA; MALT = mucosa associated lymphoid tissue.

[32] reported a significant diagnostic yield and/or better preserved specimens when TBBs were performed under general anesthesia using larger forceps and without pulling them through the biopsy channel. A similar technique (the so-called non-pull-through technique) was described by Mullins et al. [61] in small children. Using a plastic suction catheter as the working channel through which biopsy forceps are introduced and an ultrathin (2.2 mm) flexible fiberoptic bronchoscope to direct the catheter visually into the desired lung segment the authors obtained adequate tissue in eight of nine procedures (89%). Specimens obtained through TBB should be serially sectioned and immunohistochemical investigations should be encouraged: Langerhans cell histiocytosis, lymphangioleiomyomatosis, sarcoidosis, carcinomatous or malignant soft tissue metastases, and lymphoproliferative disorders are better defined using specific monoclonal antibodies (CD1a, Langerhin, HMB-45, CD68, TTF-1 or CDX-2 immunohistochemical expression). In the near future probably new markers, identified on the basis of new pathogenetic hypotheses, will be useful to identify pathological lesions also in tiny specimens [62] (table 4; fig. 6). Flexible transbronchial needle aspiration and biopsy, a minimally invasive bronchoscopic procedure increasingly used in the diagnosis and staging of lung cancer, and recently done after endobronchial ultrasonographic inspection, has a role in the diagnosis of diffuse lung diseases when they involve mediastinal lymph nodes at the onset or during the course of the illness (sarcoidosis, mycobacterioses, lymphoproliferative disorders) [36, 63]. Surgical lung biopsy can be carried out via a minithoracotomy or during a VATS. The second option is preferred today because of the larger quantity of tissue obtained and the ability to take biopsy specimens from more than one site [64, 65]. It also reduces the duration of chest tube drainage and length of hospital stay. However, it is not feasible in the presence of severe pleural adhesions or a fused pleural space, or in patients that are not able to tolerate one-lung ventilation. It seems that open lung biopsy for the diagnosis of diffuse lung disease does not.

deleteriously affect lung function whether carried out by cific fibrotic or inflammatory changes. Two specimens should be taken from two different lobes; that is, one from an area of obvious but mild abnormality and one from an area that appears almost normal. Zones of obvious severe fibrotic reaction (honeycomb pattern on HRCT scan) should not be biopsied because the lesions found there would not be helpful in the differential diagnosis.

1. The site of biopsy should be chosen taking into account the HRCT features. Gaensler and Carrington confirmed that the lingual and middle lobe tip should be avoided because they often show nonspecific fibrosis.
2. This issue is still controversial; two more recent studies which specifically addressed this question did not find any reason to avoid lingular and middle lobe biopsy.
3. Today the points that should be stressed are that HRCT helps clinicians to select the site to biopsy and lobe tips should be avoided since these sites may show nonspe-.

A surgical lung biopsy is necessary for a confident clinicopathological diagnosis of IPF/usual interstitial pneumonia.

pages.md:
monitis in cases with atypical HRCT scan findings, of the NSIP-variant pattern (in the majority of cases of NSIP-cellular variant) or of desquamative interstitial pneumonitis in cases of idiopathic lymphocytic interstitial pneumonitis, to confirm a diagnosis of idiopathic constrictive bronchiolitis, neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia and fibrous bronchiolitis or in rarer forms of infiltrative diffuse lung disease when other procedures have not made possible a definitive diagnosis [11]. Mediastinoscopy is now rarely used in the diagnosis workup of infiltrative diffuse lung diseases. It requires experienced surgeons for optimal results. This procedure is indicated in cases in which mediastinal lymphoadenopathies are evident and when other less invasive procedures have failed. Morbidity and mortality are low but not negligible.

# Conclusions

The invasive procedures available during the workup diagnosis of diffuse infiltrative lung diseases are numerous. There are still controversies over the role of these different procedures and the timing of their utilization. However, after the introduction in clinical practice of HRCT scan, the evaluation of imaging patterns, along with the immunological status of the patient and the clinical course of the disease (acute vs. chronic) seem to be crucial to solve the questions mentioned above. An algorithm useful for the practical approach to patients with supposed diffuse infiltrative lung disease is depicted in figure 7.
